html:
Roof Inspections That Produce Records, Not Guesswork

A useful roof inspection produces a document a facilities manager can act on rather than a verbal summary that fades after the walk ends. We walk the entire roof, photograph every problem area, and hand over a report formatted for capital planning, warranty compliance, or insurance renewal.

What Gets Checked on Every Visit

A full inspection covers the membrane field, all seams, every penetration and curb flashing, perimeter edge metal, and drainage points. On roofs with rooftop mechanical equipment, we also check curb seals and condensate line routing, since those are common leak sources that get missed by a surface-only walk.

We test seam adhesion on single-ply membranes with a probe rather than relying on a visual pass, since seam separation often isn't visible until it's already leaking.

Rooftop access points, ladders, and any fall-protection anchors also get a check during the walk, since a facility's rooftop safety equipment is as much a part of the maintenance picture as the membrane itself. We note any anchor point that looks corroded or loose so it can be addressed before it's relied on during a future service call.

Inspection Frequency Tied to Building Type

A distribution center along the I-85 corridor with heavy rooftop unit density needs a different inspection cadence than a low-traffic office roof. We recommend inspection frequency based on roof age, membrane type, and how much rooftop equipment traffic the building sees, rather than applying one schedule to every property in a portfolio.

Manufacturer warranties on newer systems often require documented inspections at set intervals to stay valid. We flag that requirement during the first visit so a facility doesn't lose warranty coverage over a missed inspection window.

Portfolio owners managing several properties across the county sometimes prefer a staggered inspection calendar, so every roof isn't due in the same month. We can set that schedule up front and track it across a portfolio rather than treating each building as a standalone request.

Older Buildings Need a Different Inspection Approach

Mill-era buildings with aged built-up roofs need closer attention to flashing details, since bitumen plies dry out and crack well before the field membrane shows obvious wear. We spend more time at parapets, pipe penetrations, and roof-to-wall transitions on these buildings than we would on a newer single-ply roof.

On roofs with a long repair history, we also review prior patch locations for signs the fix didn't hold, since a roof with dozens of small patches is often signaling that a section replacement would cost less over time than continued spot repair.

Masonry parapets common to the mill-village buildings around Startex and Clifton also get a closer look at the wall-to-roof transition, since water working behind aging brick can travel well beyond what shows up as a roof-level leak.

What an Inspection Report Includes

  • Photo documentation of every deficiency found, tagged to a roof plan
  • Membrane, seam, and flashing condition ratings by section
  • Drainage performance notes, including any ponding areas
  • Estimated remaining service life for the current system
  • Prioritized repair recommendations with relative urgency
  • Warranty status and any compliance items outstanding

Inspections Timed Around Operations

For manufacturing and warehouse roofs, we schedule inspection walks around dock activity and any production windows that can't tolerate foot traffic overhead. Crews check in with site security or operations staff before accessing the roof, and we can work early mornings or weekends where a facility prefers rooftop access outside normal shifts.

Plants with clean-room or quality-sensitive areas below the roof sometimes require specific dust and debris precautions during any rooftop work, inspection included. We ask about those requirements up front rather than discovering them once a crew is already on the roof, since some sections may need protective covering over rooftop unit intake vents before an inspector walks nearby.

Inspections Before a Purchase, Sale, or Insurance Renewal

Property transactions and insurance renewals both benefit from a current, documented roof condition report rather than relying on an outdated file. We provide reports formatted for due diligence packages and can turn around an inspection quickly when a transaction timeline requires it.

For buildings changing hands along the industrial corridor near Greer and the I-85 exits, a pre-sale inspection often surfaces items that shift negotiation before closing rather than becoming a dispute afterward. We write those reports the same way regardless of which party requested them, so the findings hold up to review from either side.

Inspection Questions from Facilities and Property Teams

How long does a full roof inspection take?

Most single-building inspections take a few hours, depending on roof size and how much rooftop equipment needs individual attention.

How often should a commercial roof be inspected?

We generally recommend twice a year at minimum, with additional walks after any major storm event, though roof age and manufacturer warranty terms can push that frequency higher.

Can an inspection be done without disrupting operations?

Yes. We coordinate timing with the facility so rooftop access doesn't conflict with production, dock activity, or sensitive operations below.

Does the report include cost estimates for repairs?

Yes, the report prioritizes findings by urgency and includes repair scope so a facilities team can budget accordingly.

Will an inspection catch problems a warranty requires reporting?

We flag any condition that could affect warranty standing and note the manufacturer's reporting requirement so it doesn't get missed. Most of our inspection work is on roofs originally installed by another contractor, and we assess every system on its own current condition regardless of who put it up.
